title: Water ion-product (K_w)
name: Water ion-product

variables.meaning: hydrogen-ion activity — [H⁺] relative to the 1 M standard state (dimensionless)
hydroxide-ion activity — [OH⁻] relative to the 1 M standard state (dimensionless)

assumptions.claim: Activities are approximated by concentrations relative to the 1 M standard state ($a_X = [X]/c^\circ$), the ideal-dilute-solution model. This is why $K_w$ is dimensionless and written with concentrations.

summary: $K_w$ is the product of the hydrogen-ion and hydroxide-ion **activities** ($a_X = [X]/c^\circ$), so it is **dimensionless** — the machine checks that both sides reduce to the zero dimension. Because it always holds, it ties $[\mathrm{H^+}]$ and $[\mathrm{OH^-}]$ together: a weak base fixes $[\mathrm{OH^-}]$, and $K_w$ converts that to $[\mathrm{H^+}]$ and thus a pH. Its value ($1.0\times10^{-14}$ at 25 °C) is the sourced datum; what is checked here is dimensional consistency.
domain: The autoionization of water, $\mathrm{H_2O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H^+} + \mathrm{OH^-}$, at every point in any aqueous solution. At 25 °C, $K_w = 1.0\times10^{-14}$. It is the bridge that turns an $[\mathrm{OH^-}]$ into an $[\mathrm{H^+}]$ (and pOH into pH): $[\mathrm{H^+}] = K_w/[\mathrm{OH^-}]$.
